content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kenneth Rozell 
Assistant City Attorney 
300 West Third Street, Suite 300 
Oxnard, CA 93030 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-198 
Dear Mr. Rozell: 
October 26, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Oxnard Planning 
Commissioner Deidre Frank regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission 
(the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does the Act prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to short-term rentals ("STRs") of residential properties given that she lives within 500 feet 
of one or more STRs? 
2. If the Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions, may she appear 
as a member of the general public with respect to those decisions? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Yes. The Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions because 
they would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property interest in 
her residence distinguishable from their effect on the public generally. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statulory 
references are 10 the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-198 
Page No. 2 
2. If her residence is not co-owned by any individual other than an immediate family 
member, the Commissioner may appear before the City as a member of the general public with 
respect to the decisions at issue but only on matters solely related to the residence. 
FACTS 
You are an Assistant City Attorney for the City of Oxnard and the authorized representative 
of Planning Commissioner Deidre Frank. The City is currently considering whether to impose 
restrictions and regulations on STRs within the City. 
On August 16, 2016, City staff held a community meeting regarding STRs. Approximately 
300 people attended the meeting. At the meeting, City staff gave a presentation noting that STRs 
can affect the character of communities and create nuisances for neighbors, including increased 
noise, parking, traffic, and trash, and the Commissioner publicly stated her opposition to STRs.2 
The Commissioner is also the President, and a member of the Board of Directors, of the 
Mandalay Shores Community Association, Inc. ("Mandalay Shores"). The Mandalay Shores Board 
recently banned STRs within Mandalay Shores, and the Commissioner voted in favor of the ban. 
All residences within Mandalay Shores are within the City's Coastal Zone. 
The California Coastal Commission regulates land uses within the City's Coastal Zone, and 
is required to approve any regulations pertaining to the Coastal Zone. Mandalay Shores' ban on 
STRs is currently on hold pending a response by Mandalay Shores to a letter from the Coastal 
Commission and a meeting with a Coastal Commission Enforcement Officer. Mandalay Shores is 
currently a defendant in litigation filed by two of its homeowners seeking to enjoin Mandalay 
Shores' enforcement of its STR ban, and has agreed not to enforce the ban against the plaintiffs in 
that case pending a court hearing on October 7, 2016. 
The Commissioner's residence is located within Mandalay Shores and the Coastal Zone and 
within 500 feet of one or more STRs. There are approximately 53,859 residences within the City, 
and approximately 3,500 of those residences are located within the Coastal Zone. Because the City 
does not currently have a registration program for STRs, City staff is unable to precisely determine 
the number of residences within the City that are used as STRs or that have a STR located within 
500 feet. However, City staff estimates that there are between 80 and l 00 residences used as STRs 
within the City, approximately 85 percent of the STRs are within the Coastal Zone, and 
approximately 30 to 37.5 percent of the STRs are within Mandalay Shores. The Commissioner 
asserts that given the relatively small size of residential lots within Mandalay Shores, almost every 
residence within Mandalay Shores is located within 500 feet of a STR. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, 
2 Because we do not advise on past conduct, we do not address whether the Commissioner's statement of her 
opinion on STRs at the community meeting violated the Act. 


File No. A-16-198 
Page No. 3 
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. Section 87103 also identifies the financial 
interests that may give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act. Of those interests, the facts 
presented and the decision at issue implicate the following: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 
or more or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any 
position of management (Section 87 I 03(a) and (d); Regulation 18702.1 ). If Mandalay Shores is 
a .. business entity" under Section 82005, the Commissioner has a business interest in Mandalay 
Shores. 
• 
Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b); Regulation 18702.2.) The Commissioner has a real property interest in her 
residence. 
• 
Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months 
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18702.3.) If the Commissioner receives 
sufficient compensation within the relevant timeframe for her service as President of Mandalay 
Shores, she has a source-of-income interest in Mandalay Shores. 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. 
(Section 87103; Regulations l 8702(a)(5) and 18702.5.) An official always has an interest in his 
or her personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
For a financial interest not "explicitly involved"3 in the decision at issue, the financial effect 
of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation l870l(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2{a)(l.l) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's 
effect on an official's real property interest if the decision would affect real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property: If the decision would have a 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's real property interest, the financial effect 
is material. 
In its presentation at its August 16, 2016 community meeting regarding STRs, the City 
noted that STRs can affect the character of communities by creating nuisances for neighbors, 
including increased noise, parking, traffic, and trash. The Commissioner's residence is located 
within 500 feet of one or more STRs. Because that STR or those STRs may create nuisances that 
3 An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the: interest is a named 
party in, or subject of, the decision. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
financial interest, or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
(Regulation l 87O1(a).) 


File No. A-16-198 
Page No. 4 
affect the Commissioner's residence, the decisions relating to STRs would have reasonably 
foreseeable measureable impact on the Commissioner's real property interest in her residence. 
Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions at issue would have a material 
financial effect on the Commissioner's real property interest in her residence,4 and the Act prohibits 
the Commissioner from taking part in those decisions unless their effect on the Commissioner's 
residence is indistinguishable from their effect on the public generally. 
Public Generally Exception 
The Act prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the decision's effect on 
the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the 
general rule: 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant SCl,'lllent of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment." 
Therefore, in order to invoke the exception, the official must establish that a significant 
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her interest is not unique compared to the 
effect on the significant segment. 
For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 
percent of ( 1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real property, or 
residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 
18703(b).) With respect to the decisions at issue, the "significant segment of the public" is 25 
percent of the residential real property within the City. 
There are approximately 53,859 residences within the City, and 25 percent of those 
residences equals 13,465 residences; therefore, the decisions at issue must affect 13,465 residences 
to affect a significant segment of the residences within the City. While you have provided estimates 
of the number of STRs within the City, the Coastal Zone, and Mandalay Shores, and the number of 
residences within Mandalay Shores that are within 500 feet of an STR, these estimates, even if 
assumed accurate, do not establish the decisions at issue will affect a significant segment of the 
residences within the City or that there will be no unique effect on the Commissioner. Therefore, 
the Commissioner may not invoke the public generally exception. 5 
 Because we have determined that a disqualifying conflict of interest exists under the Act with respect to the 
Commissioner's real property interest in her residence, we do not further analyze her other financial interests in the 
decisions at issue. 
5 We also note that because that the decisions regarding the STRs are not limited to a need for action at a 
specific location, the public generally exception for the "limited neighborhood effects" set forth in Regulations 
18703(e)(3) does not apply. 


Appearance as a Member of the General Public 
File No. A-16-198 
Page No. 5 
Regulation 18704 sets forth the definitions of "making a decision," "participating in a 
decision," and .. using official position to attempt to influence a decision" for purposes of the Act. 
Regulation l 8704(d)(2)(A) establishes an exception to those definitions and provides as follows: 
"(d) Exceptions. Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental 
decision does not include: ... 
"(2) Appearances as a Member of the General Public. An appearance by a public 
official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its 
prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing on matters related solely 
to the his or her personal interests, including interests in: ... 
"(A) Real property owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate 
family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family[.]" 
Accordingly, the Commissioner may appear as a member of the general public before the 
City in the course of its prescribed governmental function if the Commissioner is appearing on 
matters solely related to her personal interests in her residence if her residence is entirely owned by 
her, members of her immediate family, or her and members of her immediate family. We caution 
that for purposes of the exception, the Commissioner's "personal interests in her residence" does 
not include her being a City resident, her status as a Mandalay Shores homeowner, or her role as the 
President of Mandalay Shores. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act prohibits the Commissioner from taking part in the decisions 
relating to STRs because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on her real property interest in her residence distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally. We also conclude that the Commissioner may appear before the City as a member of the 
general public with respect to those decisions on matters solely related to her residence, if her 
residence is entirely owned by her, members of her immediate family, or her and members of her 
immediate family. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

as a member of the general public with respect to those decisions?

member, the Commissioner may appear before the City as a member of the general public with 
respect to the decisions at issue but only on matters solely related to the residence.
